episode_title: #465 — More From Sam: Iran, Jihadism, Conspiracism, AI Disruption, the Manosphere, and More
summary: Sam Harris discusses geopolitical and ideological topics including the Iran war, jihadism, progressive intellectual confusion about Islamic extremism, nuclear proliferation risks, and criticisms of Trump administration messaging and competence. He argues that jihadist regimes acquiring nuclear weapons represents an existential threat that may justify military intervention, while acknowledging the risks of mission creep and collateral damage. Harris expresses concern about what he views as moral relativism on the left regarding theocratic regimes and women's rights in Muslim-majority societies.
science_topics: Nuclear weapons and mutually assured destruction doctrine
Iran's uranium enrichment and nuclear program capabilities
claims_to_note: Iran possesses approximately 400 kilograms of partially enriched uranium - factual claim about Iranian nuclear material stockpiles requiring verification
UAE has ceased funding jihadist-inspired mosques and modified student funding due to radicalization concerns - claim about Saudi Arabia and UAE counter-jihadism policies
Jihadism primarily spreads through religious indoctrination and theological beliefs rather than as a response to military intervention - contested claim about radicalization mechanisms
Palestinian curricula in UN-funded schools teach children to aspire to martyrdom - specific allegation about educational content in Palestinian territories
policy_relevance: Congressional authorization and oversight of Iran military operations - Harris criticizes lack of Congressional preparation and authorization
NATO alliance coordination and burden-sharing in Middle East operations - discussion of tensions with allies over Strait of Hormuz security
US foreign policy toward Iran nuclear program and regime change - debate over military escalation without diplomatic clarity
Tariff and trade policy impacts on allied relationships - Trump administration's use of tariffs affecting NATO cooperation
White House communication and messaging strategy during wartime - criticism of administration messaging around military operations and civilian casualties
Nuclear nonproliferation policy regarding jihadist regimes - Harris argues jihadist-controlled nuclear weapons justify military intervention
key_quotes: You can boil down the core of a sane foreign policy on this topic to that sentence. Right. If it's a jihadist regime that is within reach of nukes, send in the troops.
The thing that really makes jihadists is the perception of jihadist success, right? The thing that really created a lot of jihadists was the rise of ISIS and the birth of the Islamic State.

In the end, it has to be other Muslims who are fighting jihadists. You don't think there's any safety in your thinking around, you know, the higher up these guys get, the less likely they are to want to die, and maybe they're just sending their underlings.

nasem_matches.topic: Nuclear weapons and mutually assured destruction doctrine

publications.title: Potential Environmental Effects of Nuclear War
publications.description: In response to the buildup of U.S. and Russian nuclear arsenals during the Cold War, a series of major scientific studies conducted in the 1980s issued warnings about the potential for a "nuclear winter" scenario - the possibility that a large-scale nuclear exchange could inject massive amounts of soot and particulates into the upper atmosphere that would block incoming solar radiation and cause major ecosystem and societal disruptions. In the decades since that concept emerged, profound military, political, and technological changes have reshaped the nuclear landscape, while scientific advances have deepened the understanding of, and ability to model, Earth system processes. It is in this context that the U.S. Congress asked for this report to re-examine the potential environmental, social, and economic effects that could unfold over the weeks to decades after a nuclear war. The effects of any given nuclear exchange would depend on key processes and interactions along a causal pathway

publications.title: Laying the Foundation for New and Advanced Nuclear Reactors in the United States
publications.description: The world confronts an existential challenge in responding to climate change, resulting in an urgent need to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from all sectors of the economy. What will it take for new and advanced nuclear reactors to play a role in decarbonization? Nuclear power provides a significant portion of the worlds low-carbon electricity, and advanced nuclear technologies have the potential to be smaller, safer, less expensive to build, and better integrated with the modern grid. However, if the United States wants advanced nuclear reactors to play a role in its plans for decarbonization, there are many key challenges that must be overcome at the technical, economic, and regulatory levels. Laying the Foundation for New and Advanced Nuclear Reactors in the United States discusses how the United States could support the successful commercialization of advanced nuclear reactors with a set of near-term policies and practices. publications.title: Accomplishments of the U.S. Global Change Research Program
publications.description: The U.S. Global Change Research Program (USGCRP) is an interagency program, established by the Global Change Research Act (GCRA) of 1990, mandated by Congress to "assist the Nation and the world to understand, assess, predict, and respond to human-induced and natural processes of global change". Since the USGCRP began, scientific understanding of global change has increased and the information needs of the nation have changed dramatically. A better understanding of what is changing and why can help decision makers in the public and private sectors cope with ongoing change. Accomplishments of the U.S. Global Change Research Program highlights the growth of global change science in the quarter century that the USGCRP has been in existence, and documents some of its contributions to that growth through its primary functions of interagency planning and coordination, and of synthesis of research and practice to inform decision making.
publications.committee: Board on Environmental Change and Society
